title: Ditching the Dense Layers
concept: Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) replace fully connected dense layers with 1x1 convolutions to output 2D spatial maps rather than single class vectors.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between semantic and instance segmentation?

options.text: Semantic groups all same-class pixels together, while instance separates individual objects
Semantic uses bounding boxes, while instance uses circles
Semantic only runs on black and white photos

exercises.explain: Correct! Semantic segmentation does not distinguish between individual instances of the same class.

cards.title: Losing the map
cards.body: In standard classification CNNs, the last layers are 'dense' (fully connected) layers. They take features, flatten them into a long vector, and output a class. But flattening destroys spatial info! If we flatten, how does the model know which pixel was where? We need a way to keep the spatial grid.

cards.title: Fully Convolutional Network
cards.body: A Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) is a neural network architecture composed entirely of convolutional layers. It replaces the final dense layers with 1x1 convolutions, allowing the output to be a 2D spatial map.

cards.title: Stamping vs. Shredding
cards.body: Flattening in a traditional CNN is like running a painting through a paper shredder to count colors. An FCN is like using a stamp: it processes the image while keeping the canvas intact, preserving where everything is located.

cards.title: From vector to grid
cards.body: A model takes a 224x224 image. A classification network flattens features to output a 1x1000 vector of classes. An FCN processes the features and outputs a 224x224 grid of predictions, mapping a class to every coordinate.

cards.title: Not just for fixed sizes
cards.body: You might think FCNs require input images of a fixed size, like classification networks. Actually, because FCNs have no dense layers (which need fixed input vectors), they can accept images of **any size** and output a matching map!

cards.title: Classification CNN vs. FCN
cards.body: Comparing the end of both networks:

left.text:
Conv layers -> Flatten -> Dense layers
Output: Single class probability vector
Destroys spatial coordinates

right.text:
Conv layers -> 1x1 Conv layers
Output: 2D Spatial Heatmap
Preserves spatial coordinates

exercises.prompt: Why do Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) replace dense layers?

options.text: To preserve the spatial coordinates and output 2D maps
To speed up training by using fewer filters
To allow the model to accept only square images
To eliminate the need for activation functions

exercises.explain: Correct! Without dense layers, FCNs retain the spatial dimensions of the features.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A Fully Convolutional Network can process input images of different resolutions.
exercises.explain: True. Because conv layers do not require a fixed input size like dense layers do, FCNs are highly flexible.

exercises.sentence: FCNs replace final fully connected layers with 1x1 ___ layers.
exercises.explain: Replacing dense layers with 1x1 convolutions preserves the spatial dimensions of the output.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a direct benefit of an FCN?

options.text: Preserving pixel location information throughout the network

exercises.explain: Preserving spatial details is the core purpose of FCNs.

exercises.prompt: You have a trained classification CNN and want to convert it to an FCN to segment images. What is the key structural change you must make?

options.text: Replace the final dense layers with 1x1 convolutional layers that match the spatial dimensions of the feature maps.
Remove all convolutional layers and keep only the dense layers.
Double the stride of every pooling layer in the network.
Add a flattening layer right before the input image.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By converting dense layers to 1x1 convolutions, we preserve the spatial grid all the way to the output. Next: how do we restore resolution after downsampling?
